Thus, they form spherical surfaces centered around the point where the net charge is zero.

subject: Physics
question_html:
Define an equipotential surface. Draw equipotential surfaces

  1. in case of a single point charge
  2. in a constant electric field in Z-direction. Why the equipotential surfaces about a single charge are not equidistant?
  3. Can electric field exist tangential to an equipotential surface? Give reason.
question_text: Define an equipotential surface. Draw equipotential surfaces in case of a single point charge in a constant electric field in Z-direction. Why the equipotential surfaces about a single charge are not equidistant? Can electric field exist tangential to an equipotential surface? Give reason.
explanation: Any surface that has the same electric potential at every point on it and no work is done to move a point charge from one point to another on it is called an equipotential surface. Equipotential surfaces in case of single point charge are concentric spheres keeping the charge at the centre. Equipotential surfaces are the parallel planes lying in the YZ-plane along the Z axis, when the electric field is in Z-direction. The equipotential surfaces due to a single point charge is represented by concentric spherical shells of increasing radius, so they are not equidistant, the separation dr between equipotential surface will go on increasing with a decrease in the electric field. No, the electric field does not exist tangentially to an equipotential surface because no work is done in moving a charge from one point to other on an equipotential surface. This indicates that the line integral of the electric field along the equipotential surface is zero. i.e. {tex}\int \vec E . \vec {dl} =0{/tex} . Hence, the equipotential surface is always perpendicular to the electric field lines at each and every point on the surface.
solution: Any surface that has the same electric potential at every point on it and no work is done to move a point charge from one point to another on it is called an equipotential surface. Equipotential surfaces in case of single point charge are concentric spheres keeping the charge at the centre. Equipotential surfaces are the parallel planes lying in the YZ-plane along the Z axis, when the electric field is in Z-direction. The equipotential surfaces due to a single point charge is represented by concentric spherical shells of increasing radius, so they are not equidistant, the separation dr between equipotential surface will go on increasing with a decrease in the electric field. No, the electric field does not exist tangentially to an equipotential surface because no work is done in moving a charge from one point to other on an equipotential surface. This indicates that the line integral of the electric field along the equipotential surface is zero. i.e. {tex}\int \vec E . \vec {dl} =0{/tex} . Hence, the equipotential surface is always perpendicular to the electric field lines at each and every point on the surface.
solution_html:
Any surface that has the same electric potential at every point on it and no work is done to move a point charge from one point to another on it is called an equipotential surface.

  1. Equipotential surfaces in case of single point charge are concentric spheres keeping the charge at the centre.
  2. Equipotential surfaces are the parallel planes lying in the YZ-plane along the Z axis, when the electric field is in Z-direction.

    The equipotential surfaces due to a single point charge is represented by concentric spherical shells of increasing radius, so they are not equidistant, the separation dr between equipotential surface will go on increasing with a decrease in the electric field.
  3. No, the electric field does not exist tangentially to an equipotential surface because no work is done in moving a charge from one point to other on an equipotential surface. This indicates that the line integral of the electric field along the equipotential surface is zero. i.e. {tex}\int \vec E . \vec {dl} =0{/tex}. Hence, the equipotential surface is always perpendicular to the electric field lines at each and every point on the surface.

question_html: Two charges {tex}5 \times {10^{ - 8}}C{/tex} and {tex} - 3 \times {10^{ - 8}}C{/tex} are located 16 cm apart. At what points on the line joining the two charges in the electric potential zero? Take the potential at infinity to be zero.
question_text: Two charges {tex}5 \times {10^{ - 8}}C{/tex} and {tex} - 3 \times {10^{ - 8}}C{/tex} are located 16 cm apart. At what points on the line joining the two charges in the electric potential zero? Take the potential at infinity to be zero.
explanation: Two charges q A = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C and q B = -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C Distance between two charges, r = 16 cm = 0.16 cm Consider a point O on the line joining two charges where the electric potential is zero due to two charges. From the figure we can see that, x = distance of point O from charge q A Electric potential at point O due to q A , {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{A}}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(\mathrm{AO})}{/tex} {tex}=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-8}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{450}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Electric potential at point O due to q B {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{B}}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(\mathrm{BO})}{/tex} {tex}=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{-3 \times 10^{-8}}{0.16-\mathrm{x}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-270}{0.16-X}{/tex} Since the total electric potential at O is zero, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V A + V B = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{450}{x}+\left(-\frac{270}{0.16-x}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{450}{x}=\frac{270}{0.16-x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5}{x}=\frac{3}{0.16-x}{/tex} On cross multiplying we get, 5 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.16 - x) = 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = 0.8 - 5x = 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8x = 0.8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0.1m = 10cm (from charge q A ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} at a distance of 10cm from the positive charge, the potential is zero between the two charges.
solution: Two charges q A = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C and q B = -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 C Distance between two charges, r = 16 cm = 0.16 cm Consider a point O on the line joining two charges where the electric potential is zero due to two charges. From the figure we can see that, x = distance of point O from charge q A Electric potential at point O due to q A , {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{A}}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(\mathrm{AO})}{/tex} {tex}=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-8}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{450}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Electric potential at point O due to q B {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{B}}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(\mathrm{BO})}{/tex} {tex}=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{-3 \times 10^{-8}}{0.16-\mathrm{x}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-270}{0.16-X}{/tex} Since the total electric potential at O is zero, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V A + V B = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{450}{x}+\left(-\frac{270}{0.16-x}\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{450}{x}=\frac{270}{0.16-x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5}{x}=\frac{3}{0.16-x}{/tex} On cross multiplying we get, 5 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.16 - x) = 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = 0.8 - 5x = 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8x = 0.8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0.1m = 10cm (from charge q A ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} at a distance of 10cm from the positive charge, the potential is zero between the two charges.
solution_html:
Two charges qA = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8C and qB = -3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8C
Distance between two charges, r = 16 cm = 0.16 cm
Consider a point O on the line joining two charges where the electric potential is zero due to two charges.

From the figure we can see that, x = distance of point O from charge qA
Electric potential at point O due to qA,
{tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{A}}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(\mathrm{AO})}{/tex}
{tex}=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-8}}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{450}{\mathrm{x}}{/tex}
Electric potential at point O due to qB
{tex}\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{B}}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(\mathrm{BO})}{/tex}
{tex}=9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{-3 \times 10^{-8}}{0.16-\mathrm{x}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-270}{0.16-X}{/tex}
Since the total electric potential at O is zero,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} VA + VB = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{450}{x}+\left(-\frac{270}{0.16-x}\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{450}{x}=\frac{270}{0.16-x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{5}{x}=\frac{3}{0.16-x}{/tex}
On cross multiplying we get,
5 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.16 - x) = 3x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = 0.8 - 5x = 3x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8x = 0.8
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0.1m = 10cm (from charge qA)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} at a distance of 10cm from the positive charge, the potential is zero between the two charges.

question_text: Determine the electrostatic potential energy of a system consisting of two charges 7 {tex}\mu{/tex} C and -2 {tex}\mu{/tex} C (and with no external field) placed at (-9 cm, 0, 0) and (9 cm, 0, 0) respectively. How much work is required to separate the two charges infinitely away from each other? Suppose that the same system of charges is now placed in an external electric field E = A ( {tex}\frac{1}{r^2}{/tex} ); A = 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 NC -1 m 2 . What would the electrostatic energy of the configuration be?
explanation: q 1 = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C, q 2 = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 C, distance between the charges is, r = 9 - (-9) = 18 cm U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r}{/tex} = {tex}9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{7 \times(-2) \times 10^{-12}}{0.18}{/tex} = -0.7 J W = U 2 - U 1 = 0 - U = 0 - (-0.7) = 0.7 J The mutual interaction energy of the two charges remains unchanged. In addition, there is the energy of interaction of the two charges with the external electric field. We find, q 1 V(r 1 ) + q 2 V(r 2 ) = {tex}A \frac{7 \mu C}{0.09 }+A \frac{-2 \mu C}{0.09 }{/tex} and the net electrostatic energy is q 1 V(r 1 ) + q 2 V(r 2 ) + {tex}\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r_{12}}{/tex} = {tex}A \frac{7 \mu C}{0.09 }+A \frac{-2 \mu C}{0.09 }-0.7 J{/tex} = 70 - 20 - 0.7 = 49.3 J

    U = {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{r}{/tex} = {tex}9 \times 10^{9} \times \frac{7 \times(-2) \times 10^{-12}}{0.18}{/tex} = -0.7 J
  2. W = U2 - U1 = 0 - U = 0 - (-0.7) = 0.7 J
  3. The mutual interaction energy of the two charges remains unchanged. In addition, there is the energy of interaction of the two charges with the external electric field. We find,
    q1V(r1) + q2V(r2) = {tex}A \frac{7 \mu C}{0.09 }+A \frac{-2 \mu C}{0.09 }{/tex}  and the net electrostatic energy is 
    q1V(r1) + q2V(r2) + {tex}\frac{q_{1} q_{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r_{12}}{/tex} = {tex}A \frac{7 \mu C}{0.09 }+A \frac{-2 \mu C}{0.09 }-0.7 J{/tex} 
    = 70 - 20 - 0.7 = 49.3 J

A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery. After sometime, the battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab with its thickness equal to the plate separation is inserted between the plates. How will

  1. the capacitances of the capacitor,
  2. potential difference between the plates and
  3. the energy stored in the capacitors be affected?
    Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery. After sometime, the battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab with its thickness equal to the plate separation is inserted between the plates. How will the capacitances of the capacitor, potential difference between the plates and the energy stored in the capacitors be affected? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: Say, the dielectric constant of the inserted dielectric is K. Now, after introduction of the dielectric slab in the isolated charged capacitor, The new capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitance(C) of the capacitor as {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} We know that charge remains same as before (according to the law of conservation of charge) in this phenomenon. i.e. Q = CV = C'V' Hence, {tex}V ^ { \prime } = \frac { C V } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { C V } { K C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex} Potential difference decreases and becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} times of the previous value. Energy stored initially in the capacitor, {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} Energy stored in the capacitor after inserting the dielectric slab of dielectric constant K, {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex} [ {tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex} ] where, K =dielectric constant of the medium {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex} The energy stored in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of the previous stored energy.
solution: Say, the dielectric constant of the inserted dielectric is K. Now, after introduction of the dielectric slab in the isolated charged capacitor, The new capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitance(C) of the capacitor as {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} We know that charge remains same as before (according to the law of conservation of charge) in this phenomenon. i.e. Q = CV = C'V' Hence, {tex}V ^ { \prime } = \frac { C V } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { C V } { K C }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex} Potential difference decreases and becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} times of the previous value. Energy stored initially in the capacitor, {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex} Energy stored in the capacitor after inserting the dielectric slab of dielectric constant K, {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex} [ {tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex} ] where, K =dielectric constant of the medium {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex} The energy stored in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of the previous stored energy.
solution_html:
Say, the dielectric constant of the inserted dielectric is K. Now, after introduction of the dielectric slab in the isolated charged capacitor,

  1. The new capacitance (C') becomes K times of original capacitance(C) of the capacitor as
    {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \;and\; C ^ { \prime } = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex}
  2. We know that charge remains same as before (according to the law of conservation of charge) in this phenomenon.
    i.e. Q = CV = C'V'
    Hence, {tex}V ^ { \prime } = \frac { C V } { C ^ { \prime } } = \frac { C V } { K C }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V ^ { \prime } = \frac { V } { K }{/tex} Potential difference decreases and becomes {tex}\frac{1}{K}{/tex} times of the previous value.
  3. Energy stored initially in the capacitor,
    {tex}U = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C }{/tex}
     Energy stored in the capacitor after inserting the dielectric slab of dielectric constant K,
    {tex}\therefore \quad U ^ { \prime } = \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 K C }{/tex}[{tex}\because C ^ { \prime } = K C{/tex}]
      where, K =dielectric constant of the medium
     {tex}\Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \left( \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 C } \right) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } ( U ) \Rightarrow U ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { K } \times U{/tex}
     The energy stored in the capacitor decreases and becomes {tex}\frac1K{/tex} times of the previous stored energy.

1. Explain briefly, using a proper diagram, the difference in behaviour of a conductor and a dielectric in the presence of external electric field.
  2. Define the term polarization of a dielectric and write the expression for a linear isotropic dielectric in terms of electric field.
question_text: Explain briefly, using a proper diagram, the difference in behaviour of a conductor and a dielectric in the presence of external electric field. Define the term polarization of a dielectric and write the expression for a linear isotropic dielectric in terms of electric field.
explanation: When a conductor is placed in an external electric field, the free charges present inside the conductor rearrange themselves in such a manner that the electric field due to induced charges opposes the external field within the conductor. After some time a static situation is achieved when the two fields cancel each other and the net electrostatic field in the conductor becomes zero. Dielectrics are non-conducting in nature, i.e. they have no free charge carriers. Thus, in a dielectric, free movement of charges is no possible. In Dielectrics the external field induces dipole moment by stretching molecules of the dielectric. The collective effect of all the molecular dipole moments creates the net charge on the surface of the dielectric which produces a field that is the net electrostatic field in a dielectric is non-zero. The dipole moment per unit volume of a polarized dielectric is known as the polarization of a dielectric. It is denoted by the letter P for the linear isotropic dielectric. The polarisation of a linear isotropic dielectric, {tex}P=\chi_e E{/tex} Where {tex}\chi_e{/tex} is the electric susceptibility of the dielectric medium.

Find the values of the capacitance of the capacitors in the two cases.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor has plate area A and plate separation d. Half of the space between the plates is filled with a material of dielectric constant K in two ways as shown in the figure. Find the values of the capacitance of the capacitors in the two cases.
explanation: {tex} \frac{1}{C} =\frac{1}{K\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}\right)}+\frac{1}{\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{C} =\frac{d}{2 K \varepsilon_0 A}+\frac{d}{2 \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{1}{K}+1\right) \frac{d}{2 \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex} {tex} C =\left(\frac{2 K}{K+1}\right) \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex} {tex} C =\frac{\varepsilon_0 A K}{2 d}+\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2 d} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{K+1}{2}\right) \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \frac{1}{C} =\frac{1}{K\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}\right)}+\frac{1}{\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}} {/tex} {tex} \frac{1}{C} =\frac{d}{2 K \varepsilon_0 A}+\frac{d}{2 \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{1}{K}+1\right) \frac{d}{2 \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex} {tex} C =\left(\frac{2 K}{K+1}\right) \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex} {tex} C =\frac{\varepsilon_0 A K}{2 d}+\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{2 d} {/tex} {tex} =\left(\frac{K+1}{2}\right) \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} \frac{1}{C} =\frac{1}{K\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}\right)}+\frac{1}{\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d / 2}} {/tex}

question_html: Three charges of +0.1 C each are placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle as shown in figure. If energy is supplied at the rate of 1 kW, how many days would be required to move the charge at A to a point D which is the mid-point of the line BC?
question_text: Three charges of +0.1 C each are placed at the corners of an equilateral triangle as shown in figure. If energy is supplied at the rate of 1 kW, how many days would be required to move the charge at A to a point D which is the mid-point of the line BC?
explanation: Potential at A due to charges at B and C is given by {tex}{V_A} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{1} + \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{1}{/tex} {tex} = 2 \times 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{1}{{10}} = 18 \times {10^8}volt{/tex} Potential at D due to charges at B and C is given by {tex}{V_D} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{{0.5}} + \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{{0.5}}{/tex} {tex} = 2 \times 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex} = 36 \times {10^8}V{/tex} Now, V D - V A {tex} = (36 - 18) \times {10^8} = 18 \times {10^8}V{/tex} Work done in moving charge 0.1 C from A to D, W = V. q {tex}W = 0.1 \times 18 \times {10^8} = 1.8 \times {10^8}J{/tex} We know that {tex}Power = \frac{{Work}}{{Time}}{/tex} or {tex}Time = \frac{{Work}}{{Power}}{/tex} Time taken to move the charge from A to D, {tex} = \frac{{1.8 \times {{10}^8}J}}{{1k\;W}} = \frac{{1.8 \times {{10}^8}J}}{{{{10}^3}J{s^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 1.8 \times 10^5 s=\frac{{1.8 \times {{10}^5}}}{{3600}}h{/tex} = 50 h {tex} = \frac{{50}}{{24}}{/tex} = 2.08 days
solution: Potential at A due to charges at B and C is given by {tex}{V_A} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{1} + \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{1}{/tex} {tex} = 2 \times 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{1}{{10}} = 18 \times {10^8}volt{/tex} Potential at D due to charges at B and C is given by {tex}{V_D} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{{0.5}} + \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{{0.5}}{/tex} {tex} = 2 \times 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex} = 36 \times {10^8}V{/tex} Now, V D - V A {tex} = (36 - 18) \times {10^8} = 18 \times {10^8}V{/tex} Work done in moving charge 0.1 C from A to D, W = V. q {tex}W = 0.1 \times 18 \times {10^8} = 1.8 \times {10^8}J{/tex} We know that {tex}Power = \frac{{Work}}{{Time}}{/tex} or {tex}Time = \frac{{Work}}{{Power}}{/tex} Time taken to move the charge from A to D, {tex} = \frac{{1.8 \times {{10}^8}J}}{{1k\;W}} = \frac{{1.8 \times {{10}^8}J}}{{{{10}^3}J{s^{ - 1}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 1.8 \times 10^5 s=\frac{{1.8 \times {{10}^5}}}{{3600}}h{/tex} = 50 h {tex} = \frac{{50}}{{24}}{/tex} = 2.08 days
solution_html:
Potential at A due to charges at B and C is given by
{tex}{V_A} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{1} + \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{1}{/tex}
{tex} = 2 \times 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{1}{{10}} = 18 \times {10^8}volt{/tex}
Potential at D due to charges at B and C is given by
{tex}{V_D} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{{0.5}} + \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\frac{{0.1}}{{0.5}}{/tex}
{tex} = 2 \times 9 \times {10^9} \times \frac{1}{5}{/tex}
{tex} = 36 \times {10^8}V{/tex}
Now, VD - VA {tex} = (36 - 18) \times {10^8} = 18 \times {10^8}V{/tex}
Work done in moving charge 0.1 C from A to D, W = V. q
{tex}W = 0.1 \times 18 \times {10^8} = 1.8 \times {10^8}J{/tex}
We know that
{tex}Power = \frac{{Work}}{{Time}}{/tex} or {tex}Time = \frac{{Work}}{{Power}}{/tex}
Time taken to move the charge from A to D,
{tex} = \frac{{1.8 \times {{10}^8}J}}{{1k\;W}} = \frac{{1.8 \times {{10}^8}J}}{{{{10}^3}J{s^{ - 1}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = 1.8 \times 10^5 s=\frac{{1.8 \times {{10}^5}}}{{3600}}h{/tex} = 50 h {tex} = \frac{{50}}{{24}}{/tex}
= 2.08 days

    Initial capacitance, C = 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−11 F
    New capacitance, C1 = kC = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−11 F = 108 pF
    New charge, q1 = C1V = 108 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 pC = 1.08 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–8 C
    Potential across the plates remains 100 V.
  2. Dielectric constant, k = 6

question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor, each with plate area A and separation d is charged to a potential difference V. The battery used to charge it remains connected. A dielectric slab of thickness d and dielectric constant K is now placed between the plates. What change if any will take place in

  1. charge on plates?
  2. electric field intensity between the plates?
  3. capacitance of the capacitor? Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor, each with plate area A and separation d is charged to a potential difference V. The battery used to charge it remains connected. A dielectric slab of thickness d and dielectric constant K is now placed between the plates. What change if any will take place in charge on plates? electric field intensity between the plates? capacitance of the capacitor? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: On introduction of dielectric slab of dielectric constant K to fill the gap between plates of capacitor of separation d completely and also connected with a battery, the given quantities become as follows: The potential difference V between the plates of capacitor remains same due to connectivity with battery and hence, charge q' becomes K times of original charge as q' = C'V' = (KC)(V) = K(CV) = Kq => q' = Kq Electric field intensity remains same as potential difference and distance of separation between two plates remain unaffected as E = {tex}\frac Vd{/tex} The capacitance of the capacitor becomes K times of original capacitance of the capacitor. {tex}\therefore \quad C ^ { \prime } = K C = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex}
solution: On introduction of dielectric slab of dielectric constant K to fill the gap between plates of capacitor of separation d completely and also connected with a battery, the given quantities become as follows: The potential difference V between the plates of capacitor remains same due to connectivity with battery and hence, charge q' becomes K times of original charge as q' = C'V' = (KC)(V) = K(CV) = Kq => q' = Kq Electric field intensity remains same as potential difference and distance of separation between two plates remain unaffected as E = {tex}\frac Vd{/tex} The capacitance of the capacitor becomes K times of original capacitance of the capacitor. {tex}\therefore \quad C ^ { \prime } = K C = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex}
solution_html:
On introduction of dielectric slab of dielectric constant K to fill the gap between plates of capacitor of separation d completely and also connected with a battery, the given quantities become as follows:

  1. The potential difference V between the plates of capacitor remains same due to connectivity with battery and hence, charge q' becomes K times of original charge as
     q' = C'V' = (KC)(V) = K(CV) = Kq
    => q' = Kq
  2. Electric field intensity remains same as potential difference and distance of separation between two plates remain unaffected as
    E = {tex}\frac Vd{/tex}
  3. The capacitance of the capacitor becomes K times of original capacitance of the capacitor.
    {tex}\therefore \quad C ^ { \prime } = K C = \frac { K \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex}

question_html: Explain, using suitable diagrams, the difference in the behaviour of a (i) conductor and (ii) dielectric in the presence of the external electric field. Define the term polarization of a dielectric and write its relation with susceptibility.
question_text: Explain, using suitable diagrams, the difference in the behaviour of a (i) conductor and (ii) dielectric in the presence of the external electric field. Define the term polarization of a dielectric and write its relation with susceptibility.
explanation: Dielectrics. In insulators, the electrons remain attached to the individual atoms or molecules. However, these electrons can suffer small movements within the atoms or molecules under the influence of an external electric field. The net effect of these microscopic movements gives rise to some important electric properties to such materials. In view of these electrical properties, insulators are called dielectrics. Polarisation of a dielectric. The induced dipole moment set up per unit volume of a dielectric when placed in an external electric field is called polarisation. For linear isotropic dielectrics, {tex}\vec{P}=\varepsilon_{0} \chi_{e} \vec{E}{/tex} where {tex}\chi_e{/tex} is the electric susceptibility of the dielectric medium.

question_html:
A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery. After some time the battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab with its thickness equal to the plate separation is inserted between the plates. What change, in any will take place in

  1. charge on the plates
  2. electric field intensity between the plates
  3. the capacitance of the capacitor,
  4. a potential difference between the plates and
  5. the energy stored in the capacitor? Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery. After some time the battery is disconnected and a dielectric slab with its thickness equal to the plate separation is inserted between the plates. What change, in any will take place in charge on the plates electric field intensity between the plates the capacitance of the capacitor, a potential difference between the plates and the energy stored in the capacitor? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: The charge q 0 on the capacitor plates remains the same because the battery has been disconnected, before placing the dielectric slab. The surface charges induced on the dielectric slab reduce electric field intensity to a new value given by {tex}E=\frac{E_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex} The reduction in the electric field induces the potential difference {tex}V=E d=\frac{E_{0} d}{k}=\frac{V_{0}}{k}{/tex} Due to the decrease in p.d., the capacitance increases k times {tex}C=\frac{q_{0}}{V}=\frac{q_{0}}{V_{0} / \mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{K} \frac{q_{0}}{V_{0}}=\kappa \mathrm{C}_{0}{/tex} Energy stored decreases by a factor of {tex}\kappa{/tex} : {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\kappa C_{0}\right)\left(\frac{V_{0}}{K}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_{0} V_{0}^{2}=\frac{U_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex}
solution: The charge q 0 on the capacitor plates remains the same because the battery has been disconnected, before placing the dielectric slab. The surface charges induced on the dielectric slab reduce electric field intensity to a new value given by {tex}E=\frac{E_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex} The reduction in the electric field induces the potential difference {tex}V=E d=\frac{E_{0} d}{k}=\frac{V_{0}}{k}{/tex} Due to the decrease in p.d., the capacitance increases k times {tex}C=\frac{q_{0}}{V}=\frac{q_{0}}{V_{0} / \mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{K} \frac{q_{0}}{V_{0}}=\kappa \mathrm{C}_{0}{/tex} Energy stored decreases by a factor of {tex}\kappa{/tex} : {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\kappa C_{0}\right)\left(\frac{V_{0}}{K}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_{0} V_{0}^{2}=\frac{U_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The charge q0 on the capacitor plates remains the same because the battery has been disconnected, before placing the dielectric slab.
  2. The surface charges induced on the dielectric slab reduce electric field intensity to a new value given by {tex}E=\frac{E_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex}
  3. The reduction in the electric field induces the potential difference {tex}V=E d=\frac{E_{0} d}{k}=\frac{V_{0}}{k}{/tex}
  4. Due to the decrease in p.d., the capacitance increases k times {tex}C=\frac{q_{0}}{V}=\frac{q_{0}}{V_{0} / \mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{K} \frac{q_{0}}{V_{0}}=\kappa \mathrm{C}_{0}{/tex}
  5. Energy stored decreases by a factor of {tex}\kappa{/tex}:
    {tex}U=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\kappa C_{0}\right)\left(\frac{V_{0}}{K}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{\kappa} \cdot \frac{1}{2} C_{0} V_{0}^{2}=\frac{U_{0}}{\kappa}{/tex}

question_html: Show that the potential energy of a dipole making angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of the field is given by {tex}U(\theta)=-\vec{P} \cdot \vec{E}{/tex}. Hence find out the amount of work done in rotating it from the position of unstable equilibrium to the stable equilibrium.
question_text: Show that the potential energy of a dipole making angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of the field is given by {tex}U(\theta)=-\vec{P} \cdot \vec{E}{/tex} . Hence find out the amount of work done in rotating it from the position of unstable equilibrium to the stable equilibrium.
explanation: The potential energy of an electric dipole in an electric field is defined as the work done in bringing the dipole from infinity to its present pasition in the electric field. Suppose the dipole is brought from infinity and placed at orientation {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of electric field. The work done in this process may be supposed to be done in two parts. The work done (W 1 ) in bringing the dipole perpendicular to electric field from infinity. Work done (W 2 ) in rotating the dipole such that it finally makes an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} from the direction of electric field. Let us suppose that the electric dipole is brought from infinity in the region of a uniform electric field such that its dipole moment {tex}\vec{P}{/tex} always remains perpendicular to electric field. The electric forces an charges +q and -q are qE and qE, along the field direction and opposite to field direction respectively. As charges +q and -q traverse equal distance under equal and opposite forces; therefore, net work done in bringing the dipole in the region of electric field perpendicular to field-direction will be zero, i.e., W 1 = 0. Now the dipole is rotated and brought to orientation making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field direction (i.e., {tex}\theta_0{/tex} = 90 o and {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = {tex}\theta^\circ{/tex} ), therefore, work done W 2 = pE (cos {tex}\theta^\circ{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} ) = pE (cos 90 o - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = -pE cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in bringing the electric dipole from infinity, i.e., Electric potential energy of electric dipole U = W 1 + W 2 = 0 - pE cos {tex}\theta^\circ{/tex} = pE cos {tex}\theta{/tex} In vector form {tex}U=-\vec{p} \cdot \vec{E}{/tex} For rotating dipole from position of unstable equilibrium ( {tex}\theta_\circ{/tex} = 18 o ) to the stable equilibrium ( {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} W req = pE (cos 180 o - cos 0 o ) pE(-1 -1) = -2pE
solution: The potential energy of an electric dipole in an electric field is defined as the work done in bringing the dipole from infinity to its present pasition in the electric field. Suppose the dipole is brought from infinity and placed at orientation {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of electric field. The work done in this process may be supposed to be done in two parts. The work done (W 1 ) in bringing the dipole perpendicular to electric field from infinity. Work done (W 2 ) in rotating the dipole such that it finally makes an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} from the direction of electric field. Let us suppose that the electric dipole is brought from infinity in the region of a uniform electric field such that its dipole moment {tex}\vec{P}{/tex} always remains perpendicular to electric field. The electric forces an charges +q and -q are qE and qE, along the field direction and opposite to field direction respectively. As charges +q and -q traverse equal distance under equal and opposite forces; therefore, net work done in bringing the dipole in the region of electric field perpendicular to field-direction will be zero, i.e., W 1 = 0. Now the dipole is rotated and brought to orientation making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field direction (i.e., {tex}\theta_0{/tex} = 90 o and {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = {tex}\theta^\circ{/tex} ), therefore, work done W 2 = pE (cos {tex}\theta^\circ{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} ) = pE (cos 90 o - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = -pE cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in bringing the electric dipole from infinity, i.e., Electric potential energy of electric dipole U = W 1 + W 2 = 0 - pE cos {tex}\theta^\circ{/tex} = pE cos {tex}\theta{/tex} In vector form {tex}U=-\vec{p} \cdot \vec{E}{/tex} For rotating dipole from position of unstable equilibrium ( {tex}\theta_\circ{/tex} = 18 o ) to the stable equilibrium ( {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o ) {tex}\therefore{/tex} W req = pE (cos 180 o - cos 0 o ) pE(-1 -1) = -2pE
solution_html:
The potential energy of an electric dipole in an electric field is defined as the work done in bringing the dipole from infinity to its present pasition in the electric field.
Suppose the dipole is brought from infinity and placed at orientation {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of electric field. The work done in this process may be supposed to be done in two parts.

  1. The work done (W1) in bringing the dipole perpendicular to electric field from infinity.
  2. Work done (W2) in rotating the dipole such that it finally makes an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} from the direction of electric field.

    Let us suppose that the electric dipole is brought from infinity in the region of a uniform electric field such that its dipole moment {tex}\vec{P}{/tex}always remains perpendicular to electric field. The electric forces an charges +q and -q are qE and qE, along the field direction and opposite to field direction respectively.

    As charges +q and -q traverse equal distance under equal and opposite forces; therefore, net work done in bringing the dipole in the region of electric field perpendicular to field-direction will be zero, i.e., W1 = 0.
    Now the dipole is rotated and brought to orientation making an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the field direction
    (i.e., {tex}\theta_0{/tex} = 90o and {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = {tex}\theta^\circ{/tex}), therefore, work done W2 = pE (cos {tex}\theta^\circ{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex}) = pE (cos 90o - cos {tex}\theta{/tex}) = -pE cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in bringing the electric dipole from infinity, i.e., Electric potential energy of electric dipole
    U = W1 + W2 = 0 - pE cos {tex}\theta^\circ{/tex} = pE cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
    In vector form {tex}U=-\vec{p} \cdot \vec{E}{/tex}
    For rotating dipole from position of unstable equilibrium ({tex}\theta_\circ{/tex} = 18o) to the stable equilibrium ({tex}\theta{/tex} = 0o)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Wreq = pE (cos 180o - cos 0o)
    pE(-1 -1) = -2pE

question_html: Briefly explain the principle of a capacitor. Derive an expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor, whose plates are separated by a dielectric medium.
question_text: Briefly explain the principle of a capacitor. Derive an expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor, whose plates are separated by a dielectric medium.
explanation: Principle of a Capacitor: A capacitor works on the principle that the capacitance of a conductor increases appreciably when an earthed conductor is brought near it. Parallel Plate Capacitor: Consider a parallel plate capacitor having two plane metallic plates A and B, placed parallel to each other (see fig.). The plates carry equal and opposite charges +Q and -Q respectively. In general, the electric field between the plates due to charges +Q and -Q remains uniform, but at the edges, the electric field lines deviate outward. If the separation between the plates is much smaller than the size of plates, the electric field strength between the plates may be assumed uniform. Let A be the area of each plate, 'd' the separation between the plates, K the dielectric constant of medium between the plates. If {tex}\sigma{/tex} is the magnitude of charge density of plates, then {tex} \sigma=\frac{Q}{A}{/tex} The electric field strength between the plates {tex} E=\frac{\sigma}{K \epsilon_0}{/tex} where {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} = permittivity of free space. ...(i) The potential difference between the plates, {tex}V_{A B}=E d=\frac{\sigma d}{K \varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(ii) Putting the value of {tex}\sigma{/tex} , we get {tex} V_{A B}=\frac{(\frac Q A) d}{K \varepsilon_0}=\frac{\sigma d}{K \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacitance of capacitor, {tex} C=\frac{Q}{V_{A B}}=\frac{Q}{\left(\frac {Qd}{ K \varepsilon_0 A}\right)} {/tex} or C {tex}= \frac{K \varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex} ...(iii) This is a general expression for capacitance of parallel plate capacitor. Obviously, the capacitance is directly proportional to the dielectric constant of medium between the plates. For air capacitor (K = 1); capacitance {tex}C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}{/tex} . This is expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate air capacitor. It can be seen that the capacitance of parallel plate (air) capacitor is: directly proportional to the area of each plate. inversely proportional to the distance between the plates. independent of the material of the plates.

Parallel Plate Capacitor: Consider a parallel plate capacitor having two plane metallic plates A and B, placed parallel to each other (see fig.). The plates carry equal and opposite charges +Q and -Q respectively. In general, the electric field between the plates due to charges +Q and -Q remains uniform, but at the edges, the electric field lines deviate outward. If the separation between the plates is much smaller than the size of plates, the electric field strength between the plates may be assumed uniform. Let A be the area of each plate, 'd' the separation between the plates, K the dielectric constant of medium between the plates. If {tex}\sigma{/tex} is the magnitude of charge density of plates, then {tex} \sigma=\frac{Q}{A}{/tex} The electric field strength between the plates {tex} E=\frac{\sigma}{K \epsilon_0}{/tex} where {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} = permittivity of free space. ...(i) The potential difference between the plates, {tex}V_{A B}=E d=\frac{\sigma d}{K \varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(ii) Putting the value of {tex}\sigma{/tex} , we get {tex} V_{A B}=\frac{(\frac Q A) d}{K \varepsilon_0}=\frac{\sigma d}{K \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacitance of capacitor, {tex} C=\frac{Q}{V_{A B}}=\frac{Q}{\left(\frac {Qd}{ K \varepsilon_0 A}\right)} {/tex} or C {tex}= \frac{K \varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex} ...(iii) This is a general expression for capacitance of parallel plate capacitor. Obviously, the capacitance is directly proportional to the dielectric constant of medium between the plates. For air capacitor (K = 1); capacitance {tex}C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}{/tex} . This is expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate air capacitor. It can be seen that the capacitance of parallel plate (air) capacitor is: directly proportional to the area of each plate. inversely proportional to the distance between the plates. independent of the material of the plates.
solution_html:
Principle of a Capacitor: A capacitor works on the principle that the capacitance of a conductor increases appreciably when an earthed conductor is brought near it.
Parallel Plate Capacitor: Consider a parallel plate capacitor having two plane metallic plates A and B, placed parallel to each other (see fig.). The plates carry equal and opposite charges +Q and -Q respectively.

In general, the electric field between the plates due to charges +Q and -Q remains uniform, but at the edges, the electric field lines deviate outward. If the separation between the plates is much smaller than the size of plates, the electric field strength between the plates may be assumed uniform.
Let A be the area of each plate, 'd' the separation between the plates, K the dielectric constant of medium between the plates. If {tex}\sigma{/tex} is the magnitude of charge density of plates, then
{tex} \sigma=\frac{Q}{A}{/tex}
The electric field strength between the plates
{tex} E=\frac{\sigma}{K \epsilon_0}{/tex} where {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} = permittivity of free space. ...(i)
The potential difference between the plates, {tex}V_{A B}=E d=\frac{\sigma d}{K \varepsilon_0}{/tex} ...(ii)
Putting the value of {tex}\sigma{/tex}, we get
{tex} V_{A B}=\frac{(\frac Q A) d}{K \varepsilon_0}=\frac{\sigma d}{K \varepsilon_0 A} {/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacitance of capacitor,
{tex} C=\frac{Q}{V_{A B}}=\frac{Q}{\left(\frac {Qd}{ K \varepsilon_0 A}\right)} {/tex}  or C {tex}= \frac{K \varepsilon_0 A}{d} {/tex} ...(iii)
This is a general expression for capacitance of parallel plate capacitor. Obviously, the capacitance is directly proportional to the dielectric constant of medium between the plates.
For air capacitor (K = 1); capacitance {tex}C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}{/tex}. This is expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate air capacitor. It can be seen that the capacitance of parallel plate (air) capacitor is:

  1. directly proportional to the area of each plate.
  2. inversely proportional to the distance between the plates.
  3. independent of the material of the plates.

question_html:
1. A parallel plate capacitor with plate area A and plate separation {tex}d{/tex} has a capacitance {tex}C_{0}{/tex}. A slab of dielectric constant {tex}K{/tex} having area A and thickness {tex}\left(\frac{d}{4}\right){/tex} is inserted in the capacitor, parallel to the plates. Find the new value of its capacitance.
  2. You are provided with a large number of {tex}1 \mu {~F}{/tex} identical capacitors and a power supply of 1200 V. The dielectric medium used in each capacitor can withstand up to 200 V only. Find the minimum number of capacitors and their arrangement, required to build a capacitor system of equivalent capacitance of {tex}2 \mu {~F}{/tex} for use with this supply.
question_text: A parallel plate capacitor with plate area A and plate separation {tex}d{/tex} has a capacitance {tex}C_{0}{/tex} . A slab of dielectric constant {tex}K{/tex} having area A and thickness {tex}\left(\frac{d}{4}\right){/tex} is inserted in the capacitor, parallel to the plates. Find the new value of its capacitance. You are provided with a large number of {tex}1 \mu {~F}{/tex} identical capacitors and a power supply of 1200 V. The dielectric medium used in each capacitor can withstand up to 200 V only. Find the minimum number of capacitors and their arrangement, required to build a capacitor system of equivalent capacitance of {tex}2 \mu {~F}{/tex} for use with this supply.
explanation: {tex} {C}_0 =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}} {/tex} {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{({~d}-{t})+\frac{{t}}{K}} {/tex} {tex} {t} ={d} / 4 {/tex} {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{\left({~d}-\frac{{d}}{4}\right)+\frac{{d}}{4 {~K}}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}\left(\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4 {~K}}\right)} {/tex} {tex} ={C}_0 \frac{4 {~K}}{(3 {~K}+1)} {/tex} Each capacitance can withstand 200 V No. of capacitors in each row {tex}=\frac{1200}{200}=6{/tex} Net capacitance of each row {tex}=1 / 6 \mu {~F}{/tex} Number of rows {tex}={n}{/tex} {tex} C_{e q} =C_1+C_2+------+C_n {/tex} {tex} C_{e q} =\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+-----n {/tex} {tex} 2 =\frac{n}{6} {/tex} {tex} \therefore n=12 {/tex} {tex} \text { Total no. of capacitors in the arrangement } =6 \times 12 {/tex} {tex} =72 {/tex}
solution: {tex} {C}_0 =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}} {/tex} {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{({~d}-{t})+\frac{{t}}{K}} {/tex} {tex} {t} ={d} / 4 {/tex} {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{\left({~d}-\frac{{d}}{4}\right)+\frac{{d}}{4 {~K}}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}\left(\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4 {~K}}\right)} {/tex} {tex} ={C}_0 \frac{4 {~K}}{(3 {~K}+1)} {/tex} Each capacitance can withstand 200 V No. of capacitors in each row {tex}=\frac{1200}{200}=6{/tex} Net capacitance of each row {tex}=1 / 6 \mu {~F}{/tex} Number of rows {tex}={n}{/tex} {tex} C_{e q} =C_1+C_2+------+C_n {/tex} {tex} C_{e q} =\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+-----n {/tex} {tex} 2 =\frac{n}{6} {/tex} {tex} \therefore n=12 {/tex} {tex} \text { Total no. of capacitors in the arrangement } =6 \times 12 {/tex} {tex} =72 {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} {C}_0 =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}} {/tex}
    {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{({~d}-{t})+\frac{{t}}{K}} {/tex}
    {tex} {t} ={d} / 4 {/tex}
    {tex} {C} =\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{\left({~d}-\frac{{d}}{4}\right)+\frac{{d}}{4 {~K}}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 {~A}}{{~d}\left(\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{4 {~K}}\right)} {/tex}
    {tex} ={C}_0 \frac{4 {~K}}{(3 {~K}+1)} {/tex}
  2. Each capacitance can withstand 200 V
    No. of capacitors in each row {tex}=\frac{1200}{200}=6{/tex}
    Net capacitance of each row {tex}=1 / 6 \mu {~F}{/tex}
    Number of rows {tex}={n}{/tex}
    {tex} C_{e q} =C_1+C_2+------+C_n {/tex}
    {tex} C_{e q} =\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}+-----n {/tex}
    {tex} 2 =\frac{n}{6} {/tex}
    {tex} \therefore n=12 {/tex}
    {tex} \text { Total no. of capacitors in the arrangement } =6 \times 12 {/tex}
    {tex} =72 {/tex}

question_html: Derive an expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor when a dielectric slab of dielectric constant K and thickness {tex}t=\frac{d}{2}{/tex} but of same area as that of the plates is inserted between the capacitor plates. (d = separation between the plates).
question_text: Derive an expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor when a dielectric slab of dielectric constant K and thickness {tex}t=\frac{d}{2}{/tex} but of same area as that of the plates is inserted between the capacitor plates. (d = separation between the plates).
explanation: Consider a parallel plate capacitor, area of each plate being -A, the separation between the plates being d. Let a dielectric slab of dielectric constant K and thickness t < d be placed between the plates. The thickness of air between the plates is (d - t). If charges on plates are +Q and -Q, then surface charge density {tex} \sigma=\frac{Q}{A} {/tex} The electric field between the plates in air, {tex}E_1=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}{/tex} The electric field between the plates in slab, {tex}E_2=\frac{\sigma}{K \varepsilon_0}=\frac{Q}{K \varepsilon_0 A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The potential difference between the plates V AB = work done in carrying unit positive charge from one plate to another {tex}=\Sigma E x{/tex} (as field between the plates is not constant). = E 1 (d - t) + E 2 t {tex}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}(d-t)+\frac{Q}{K \varepsilon_0 A} t {/tex} {tex}\therefore V_{A B}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}\left[d-t+\frac{t}{K}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacitance of capacitor, {tex}C=\frac{Q}{V_{A B}}=\frac{Q}{\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}\left(d-t+\frac{t}{K}\right)}{/tex} or, {tex} C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d-t+\frac{t}{K}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d-t\left(1-\frac{1}{K}\right)} {/tex} Here, {tex}t=\frac{d}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d-\frac{d}{2}\left(1-\frac{1}{K}\right)}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\frac{d}{2}\left(1-\frac{1}{K}\right)}{/tex}
solution: Consider a parallel plate capacitor, area of each plate being -A, the separation between the plates being d. Let a dielectric slab of dielectric constant K and thickness t < d be placed between the plates. The thickness of air between the plates is (d - t). If charges on plates are +Q and -Q, then surface charge density {tex} \sigma=\frac{Q}{A} {/tex} The electric field between the plates in air, {tex}E_1=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}{/tex} The electric field between the plates in slab, {tex}E_2=\frac{\sigma}{K \varepsilon_0}=\frac{Q}{K \varepsilon_0 A}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The potential difference between the plates V AB = work done in carrying unit positive charge from one plate to another {tex}=\Sigma E x{/tex} (as field between the plates is not constant). = E 1 (d - t) + E 2 t {tex}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}(d-t)+\frac{Q}{K \varepsilon_0 A} t {/tex} {tex}\therefore V_{A B}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}\left[d-t+\frac{t}{K}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacitance of capacitor, {tex}C=\frac{Q}{V_{A B}}=\frac{Q}{\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}\left(d-t+\frac{t}{K}\right)}{/tex} or, {tex} C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d-t+\frac{t}{K}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d-t\left(1-\frac{1}{K}\right)} {/tex} Here, {tex}t=\frac{d}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d-\frac{d}{2}\left(1-\frac{1}{K}\right)}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\frac{d}{2}\left(1-\frac{1}{K}\right)}{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider a parallel plate capacitor, area of each plate being -A, the separation between the plates being d. Let a dielectric slab of dielectric constant K and thickness t < d be placed between the plates. The thickness of air between the plates is (d - t). If charges on plates are +Q and -Q, then surface charge density
{tex} \sigma=\frac{Q}{A} {/tex}

The electric field between the plates in air, {tex}E_1=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}{/tex}
The electric field between the plates in slab, {tex}E_2=\frac{\sigma}{K \varepsilon_0}=\frac{Q}{K \varepsilon_0 A}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The potential difference between the plates
VAB = work done in carrying unit positive charge from one plate to another
{tex}=\Sigma E x{/tex} (as field between the plates is not constant).
 = E1(d - t) + E2t {tex}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}(d-t)+\frac{Q}{K \varepsilon_0 A} t {/tex}
{tex}\therefore V_{A B}=\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}\left[d-t+\frac{t}{K}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Capacitance of capacitor, {tex}C=\frac{Q}{V_{A B}}=\frac{Q}{\frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 A}\left(d-t+\frac{t}{K}\right)}{/tex}
or, {tex} C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d-t+\frac{t}{K}}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d-t\left(1-\frac{1}{K}\right)} {/tex}
Here, {tex}t=\frac{d}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d-\frac{d}{2}\left(1-\frac{1}{K}\right)}=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\frac{d}{2}\left(1-\frac{1}{K}\right)}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Explain why, for any charge configuration, the equipotential surface through a point is normal to the electric field at that point.
    Draw a sketch of equipotential surfaces due to a single charge (-q), depicting the electric field lines due to the charge.
  2. Obtain an expression for the work done to dissociate the system of three charges placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side a as shown alongside.
question_text: Explain why, for any charge configuration, the equipotential surface through a point is normal to the electric field at that point. Draw a sketch of equipotential surfaces due to a single charge (-q), depicting the electric field lines due to the charge. Obtain an expression for the work done to dissociate the system of three charges placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side a as shown alongside.
explanation: The work done in moving a charge from one point to another on an equipotential surface is zero. If the field is not normal to an equipotential surface, it would have a non zero component along the surface. This would imply that work would have to be done to move a charge on the surface which is contradictory to the definition of equipotential surface. Mathematically Work done to move a charge dq, on a surface, can be expressed as {tex}d W=d q(\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}){/tex} But dW = 0 on an equipotential surface {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{E} \perp \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} Equipotential surfaces for a charge -q is shown alongside. Work required to dissociate the system of three charges = - P.E. of the system {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q \times(-4 q)}{a}+\frac{q \times 2 q}{a}+\frac{(-4 q) \times 2 q}{a}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[-4 q^{2}+2 q^{2}-8 q^{2}\right]=+\frac{10 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a}{/tex}

If the field is not normal to an equipotential surface, it would have a non zero component along the surface. This would imply that work would have to be done to move a charge on the surface which is contradictory to the definition of equipotential surface. Mathematically Work done to move a charge dq, on a surface, can be expressed as {tex}d W=d q(\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}){/tex} But dW = 0 on an equipotential surface {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{E} \perp \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex} Equipotential surfaces for a charge -q is shown alongside. Work required to dissociate the system of three charges = - P.E. of the system {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q \times(-4 q)}{a}+\frac{q \times 2 q}{a}+\frac{(-4 q) \times 2 q}{a}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[-4 q^{2}+2 q^{2}-8 q^{2}\right]=+\frac{10 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The work done in moving a charge from one point to another on an equipotential surface is zero. If the field is not normal to an equipotential surface, it would have a non zero component along the surface. This would imply that work would have to be done to move a charge on the surface which is contradictory to the definition of equipotential surface.
    Mathematically
    Work done to move a charge dq, on a surface, can be expressed as {tex}d W=d q(\vec{E} \cdot \overrightarrow{d r}){/tex}
    But dW = 0 on an equipotential surface
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\vec{E} \perp \overrightarrow{d r}{/tex}
    Equipotential surfaces for a charge -q is shown alongside.
  2. Work required to dissociate the system of three charges
    = - P.E. of the system
    {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q \times(-4 q)}{a}+\frac{q \times 2 q}{a}+\frac{(-4 q) \times 2 q}{a}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[-4 q^{2}+2 q^{2}-8 q^{2}\right]=+\frac{10 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} a}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Derive an expression for potential energy of an electric dipole {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} in an external uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex}. When is the potential energy of the dipole (1) maximum, and (2) minimum?
  2. An electric dipole consists of point charges -1.0 pC and +1.0 pC located at {tex}(0,0){/tex} and (3 mm, 4 mm) respectively in x - y plane. An electric field {tex}\vec{E}=\left(\frac{1000 V}{m}\right) \hat{i}{/tex} is switched on in the region. Find the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acting on the dipole.
question_text: Derive an expression for potential energy of an electric dipole {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} in an external uniform electric field {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} . When is the potential energy of the dipole (1) maximum, and (2) minimum? An electric dipole consists of point charges -1.0 pC and +1.0 pC located at {tex}(0,0){/tex} and (3 mm, 4 mm) respectively in x - y plane. An electric field {tex}\vec{E}=\left(\frac{1000 V}{m}\right) \hat{i}{/tex} is switched on in the region. Find the torque {tex}\vec{\tau}{/tex} acting on the dipole.
explanation: The amount of work done in rotating the dipole from {tex}\theta=\theta_0{/tex} to {tex}\theta=\theta_1{/tex} by the external torque {tex}{W}=\int_\limits{e_0}^{\theta_1} \tau_{e x t} d \theta{/tex} {tex}=\int_\limits{\theta_0}^a p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex}{W}=p E\left(\cos \theta_0-\cos \theta_1\right){/tex} For {tex}\theta_0=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\theta_1=\theta{/tex} {tex}=p E\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos \theta\right){/tex} {tex}U(\theta)=-p E \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=-\vec{p} \cdot \vec{E}{/tex} Potential energy is maximum when: {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is antiparallel to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} Potential energy is minimum when: {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is along to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} Dipole Moment Calculation: The charge q = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C. Separation vector {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} = (3 mm, 4 mm) = (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m) Dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}=q \vec{d}{/tex} = (1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 , 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 ) = (3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 , 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 ) C.m. Torque Calculation: Electric field {tex}\vec{E}=1000 \hat{i} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}{/tex} Torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau}=\left(3.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{i}+4.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{j}\right) \times 1000 \hat{i}=\left(4.0 \times 10^{-15} \times 1000\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex} The torque is {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex}
solution: The amount of work done in rotating the dipole from {tex}\theta=\theta_0{/tex} to {tex}\theta=\theta_1{/tex} by the external torque {tex}{W}=\int_\limits{e_0}^{\theta_1} \tau_{e x t} d \theta{/tex} {tex}=\int_\limits{\theta_0}^a p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} {tex}{W}=p E\left(\cos \theta_0-\cos \theta_1\right){/tex} For {tex}\theta_0=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\theta_1=\theta{/tex} {tex}=p E\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos \theta\right){/tex} {tex}U(\theta)=-p E \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}=-\vec{p} \cdot \vec{E}{/tex} Potential energy is maximum when: {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is antiparallel to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} Potential energy is minimum when: {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is along to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} Dipole Moment Calculation: The charge q = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C. Separation vector {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} = (3 mm, 4 mm) = (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 m) Dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}=q \vec{d}{/tex} = (1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 , 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 ) = (3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 , 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 ) C.m. Torque Calculation: Electric field {tex}\vec{E}=1000 \hat{i} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}{/tex} Torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau}=\left(3.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{i}+4.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{j}\right) \times 1000 \hat{i}=\left(4.0 \times 10^{-15} \times 1000\right) \hat{k}{/tex} {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex} The torque is {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. The amount of work done in rotating the dipole from {tex}\theta=\theta_0{/tex} to {tex}\theta=\theta_1{/tex} by the external torque
    {tex}{W}=\int_\limits{e_0}^{\theta_1} \tau_{e x t} d \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=\int_\limits{\theta_0}^a p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex}
    {tex}{W}=p E\left(\cos \theta_0-\cos \theta_1\right){/tex}
    For {tex}\theta_0=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\theta_1=\theta{/tex}
    {tex}=p E\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos \theta\right){/tex}
    {tex}U(\theta)=-p E \cos \theta{/tex}{tex}=-\vec{p} \cdot \vec{E}{/tex}
    1. Potential energy is maximum when:
      {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is antiparallel to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex}
    2. Potential energy is minimum when:
      {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} is along to {tex}\vec{E}{/tex}
  2. Dipole Moment Calculation:
    The charge q = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12C.
    Separation vector {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} = (3 mm, 4 mm) = (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m, 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 m)
    Dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}=q \vec{d}{/tex} = (1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3, 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3) = (3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15, 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15) C.m.
    Torque Calculation:
    Electric field {tex}\vec{E}=1000 \hat{i} \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}{/tex}
    Torque {tex}\vec{\tau}=\vec{p} \times \vec{E}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{\tau}=\left(3.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{i}+4.0 \times 10^{-15} \hat{j}\right) \times 1000 \hat{i}=\left(4.0 \times 10^{-15} \times 1000\right) \hat{k}{/tex}
    {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex}
    The torque is {tex}\vec{\tau}=4.0 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~N} \cdot \mathrm{m} \hat{k}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Derive the expression for the electric potential due to an electric dipole at a point on its axial line.
  2. Depict the equipotential surfaces due to an electric dipole.
question_text: Derive the expression for the electric potential due to an electric dipole at a point on its axial line. Depict the equipotential surfaces due to an electric dipole.
explanation: Let electric potential is to be determined at a point P lying on the axis of an electric dipole of dipole length 2l at a distance d from the centre of the dipole as shown in the figure. Potential at P due to +q charge of the dipole {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { ( d - l) }{/tex} Potential at P due to -q charge of the dipole {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { - q } { ( d + l ) }{/tex} Total potential at P due to both the charges of the dipole {tex}= \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { ( d - l) } - \frac { 1 } { ( d + l ) } \right]{/tex} {tex}= q \times \frac {2l} {4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }} × \frac {1}{\left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)} = \frac {p} { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }}× \frac {1}{ \left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)}{/tex} where, the scalar value of dipole moment (p) = q × 2l If l << d, then neglecting l 2 we get, the final value of the electric potential to be, {tex}V = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { p } { d ^ { 2 } }{/tex} An electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges separated by some distance. Equipotential lines and electric field are always perpendicular to each other. Equipotential surfaces of a dipole are as shown below: Potential of points lying on the perpendicular bisector surface will be zero.
solution: Let electric potential is to be determined at a point P lying on the axis of an electric dipole of dipole length 2l at a distance d from the centre of the dipole as shown in the figure. Potential at P due to +q charge of the dipole {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { ( d - l) }{/tex} Potential at P due to -q charge of the dipole {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { - q } { ( d + l ) }{/tex} Total potential at P due to both the charges of the dipole {tex}= \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { ( d - l) } - \frac { 1 } { ( d + l ) } \right]{/tex} {tex}= q \times \frac {2l} {4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }} × \frac {1}{\left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)} = \frac {p} { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }}× \frac {1}{ \left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)}{/tex} where, the scalar value of dipole moment (p) = q × 2l If l << d, then neglecting l 2 we get, the final value of the electric potential to be, {tex}V = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { p } { d ^ { 2 } }{/tex} An electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges separated by some distance. Equipotential lines and electric field are always perpendicular to each other. Equipotential surfaces of a dipole are as shown below: Potential of points lying on the perpendicular bisector surface will be zero.
solution_html:
1. Let electric potential is to be determined at a point P lying on the axis of an electric dipole of dipole length 2l at a distance d from the centre of the dipole as shown in the figure.

    Potential at P due to +q charge of the dipole {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \cdot \frac { q } { ( d - l) }{/tex}
    Potential at P due to -q charge of the dipole
    {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { - q } { ( d + l ) }{/tex}
    Total potential at P due to both the charges of the dipole
    {tex}= \frac { q } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { ( d - l) } - \frac { 1 } { ( d + l ) } \right]{/tex}
    {tex}= q \times \frac {2l} {4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }} × \frac {1}{\left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)} = \frac {p} { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 }}× \frac {1}{ \left( d ^ { 2 } - l ^ { 2 } \right)}{/tex}
    where, the scalar value of dipole moment (p) = q × 2l
    If l << d, then neglecting l2 we get, the final value of the electric potential to be, {tex}V = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi \varepsilon _ { 0 } } \frac { p } { d ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
  2. An electric dipole consists of two equal and opposite charges separated by some distance. Equipotential lines and electric field are always perpendicular to each other. Equipotential surfaces of a dipole are as shown below:

question_text: Obtain an expression for the potential energy of an electric dipole placed in a uniform electric field. Three capacitors of capacitance C 1 , C 2 and C 3 are connected in series to a source of V volt. Show that the total energy stored in the combination of capacitors is equal to sum of the energy stored in individual capacitors. A capacitor of capacitance C is connected across a battery. After charging, the battery is disconnected and the separation between the plates is doubled. How will (i) the capacitance of the capacitor, and (ii) the electric field between the plates be affected? Justify your answer.
explanation: Consider a dipole with charges q1 = +q and q2 = -q is placed in a uniform electric field E, as shown in Fig The dipole experiences no net force; but experiences a torque {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}{/tex} given by {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}{/tex} = p {tex}\times{/tex} E which will tend to rotate it (unless p is parallel or antiparallel to E). Suppose an external torque {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext}{/tex} is applied in such a manner that it just neutralizes this torque and rotates it in the plane of paper from angle {tex}\theta_o{/tex} to angle {tex}\theta_1{/tex} at an infinitesimal angular speed and without angular acceleration. The amount of work done by the external torque will be given by W = {tex}\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_1} \tau_{\text {ext }}(\theta) d \theta{/tex} = {tex}\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_1} p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex} W = U = pE {tex}\left(\cos \theta_0-\cos \theta_1\right){/tex} U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_{\text {eff }}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{C_{\text {eff }}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}{/tex} U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_1}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_3}{/tex} U = U 1 + U 2 + U 3 When battery is disconnected then charge (q) remains constant. Capacitance is halved C' = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_o A}{2 d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{C}{2}{/tex} Electric field (E) is unaffected. E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_o A}{/tex} Alternatively for effect on electric field. E = {tex}\frac{V}{d}{/tex} V' = {tex}\frac{Q}{C^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q}{\frac{C}{ 2}}{/tex} = 2 V E' = {tex}\frac{V^{\prime}}{d^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 V}{2 d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{d}{/tex} = E

1. Consider a dipole with charges q1 = +q and q2 = -q is placed in a uniform electric field E, as shown in Fig The dipole experiences no net force; but experiences a torque {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}{/tex} given by {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}{/tex} = p {tex}\times{/tex} E 
    which will tend to rotate it (unless p is parallel or antiparallel to E). Suppose an external torque {tex}\boldsymbol{\tau}_{ext}{/tex} is applied in such a manner that it just neutralizes this torque and rotates it in the plane of paper from angle {tex}\theta_o{/tex} to angle {tex}\theta_1{/tex} at an infinitesimal angular speed and without angular acceleration. The amount of work done by the external torque will be given by
    W = {tex}\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_1} \tau_{\text {ext }}(\theta) d \theta{/tex} = {tex}\int_{\theta_0}^{\theta_1} p E \sin \theta d \theta{/tex}
    W = U = pE {tex}\left(\cos \theta_0-\cos \theta_1\right){/tex}
  2. U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_{\text {eff }}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{C_{\text {eff }}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}{/tex}
    U = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_1}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{Q^2}{C_3}{/tex}
    U = U1 + U2 + U3

  3. When battery is disconnected then charge (q) remains constant.
    Capacitance is halved
    C' = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_o A}{2 d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{C}{2}{/tex}
    Electric field (E) is unaffected.
    E = {tex}\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{q}{\varepsilon_o A}{/tex}
    Alternatively for effect on electric field.
    E = {tex}\frac{V}{d}{/tex}
    V' = {tex}\frac{Q}{C^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q}{\frac{C}{ 2}}{/tex} = 2 V
    E' = {tex}\frac{V^{\prime}}{d^{\prime}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 V}{2 d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{V}{d}{/tex} = E

1. Describe briefly the process of transferring the charge between the two plates of a parallel plate capacitor when connected to a battery. Derive an expression for the energy stored in a capacitor.
  2. A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery to a potential difference V. It is disconnected from the battery and then connected to another uncharged capacitor of the same capacitance. Calculate the ratio of the energy stored in the combination to the initial energy on the single capacitor.
question_text: Describe briefly the process of transferring the charge between the two plates of a parallel plate capacitor when connected to a battery. Derive an expression for the energy stored in a capacitor. A parallel plate capacitor is charged by a battery to a potential difference V. It is disconnected from the battery and then connected to another uncharged capacitor of the same capacitance. Calculate the ratio of the energy stored in the combination to the initial energy on the single capacitor.
explanation: Consider a parallel plate capacitor which is connected across a battery. The electrons are transferred from the negative terminal of the battery to the metallic plate connected to the negative terminal and acquires a negative charge. Similarly, the electrons move from the second plate to the positive terminal of the battery and acquire a positive charge. This process continues until the potential difference between the two plates becomes equal to the potential difference between the terminals of the battery. Thus, the charge is developed on the capacitor. Let ‘dW’ be the work done by the battery in increasing the charge on the capacitor is given by, having the charge q and potential V is: dW = V dq where V = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} dW = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex} dq Total work done in charging up the capacitor is given by, W = {tex}\int{/tex} dW = {tex}\int\limits_0^Q {\frac{q}{c}} {/tex} dq {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = {tex}\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex} Hence total energy stored in the plates of the capacitor is given by, W = {tex}\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} Q V{/tex} Charge on the plates of the capacitor is given by q = CV When uncharged capacitor of same capacitance is connected to the charged capacitor, sharing of charges takes place between the two capacitors till both the capacitors acquire same potential {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} Energy stored in the combination of capacitors is given by, {tex}U_2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C( {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} ) 2 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} C( {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex} ) 2 = {tex}\frac{CV^2}{4}{/tex} Energy stored by a single capacitor before connecting is given by, U 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} CV 2 Ratio of energy stored in the combination to that in the single capacitor is given by, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{U}_{2}}{\mathrm{U}_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{CV}^{2} / 4}{C V^{2} / 2}{/tex} = 1 : 2 , Hence these are required results.

The electrons are transferred from the negative terminal of the battery to the metallic plate connected to the negative terminal and acquires a negative charge. Similarly, the electrons move from the second plate to the positive terminal of the battery and acquire a positive charge. 1. Consider a parallel plate capacitor which is connected across a battery. The electrons are transferred from the negative terminal of the battery to the metallic plate connected to the negative terminal and acquires a negative charge. Similarly, the electrons move from the second plate to the positive terminal of the battery and acquire a positive charge. This process continues until the potential difference between the two plates becomes equal to the potential difference between the terminals of the battery. Thus, the charge is developed on the capacitor.
    Let ‘dW’ be the work done by the battery in increasing the charge on the capacitor is given by, having the charge q and potential V is:
    dW = V dq
    where V = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex}  dW = {tex}\frac{q}{C}{/tex}dq
    Total work done in charging up the capacitor is given by,
    W = {tex}\int{/tex} dW = {tex}\int\limits_0^Q {\frac{q}{c}} {/tex}dq
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} W = {tex}\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}{/tex}
    Hence total energy stored in the plates of the capacitor is given by, W = {tex}\frac{Q^{2}}{2 C}=\frac{1}{2} C V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} Q V{/tex}
  2. Charge on the plates of the capacitor is given by q = CV
    When uncharged capacitor of same capacitance is connected to the charged capacitor, sharing of charges takes place between the two capacitors till both the capacitors acquire same potential {tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex}
    Energy stored in the combination of capacitors is given by,
    {tex}U_2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}C({tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex})2 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}C({tex}\frac{V}{2}{/tex})2 = {tex}\frac{CV^2}{4}{/tex}
    Energy stored by a single capacitor before connecting is given by, U1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}CV2 
    Ratio of energy stored in the combination to that in the single capacitor is given by,
    {tex}\frac{\mathrm{U}_{2}}{\mathrm{U}_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{CV}^{2} / 4}{C V^{2} / 2}{/tex} = 1 : 2  , Hence these are required results.

question_text: Derive an expression for potential due to a dipole for distances large compared to the size of the dipole. How is the potential due to dipole different from that due to a single charge?
explanation: Consider origin at the centre of dipole. As per superposition principle, potential due to dipole will be the sum of potentials due to charges q and -q {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{r_{1}}-\frac{q}{r_{2}}\right]{/tex} Where, r 1 and r 2 = distances of point P from q and -q. r 1 2 = r 2 + a 2 - 2 arcos {tex}\theta{/tex} r 2 2 = r 2 + a 2 + 2 arcos {tex}\theta{/tex} If r is greater than a, and taking terms upto first order in a/r r 1 2 = r 2 {tex}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}+\frac{a^{2}}{r^{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=r^{2}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex} Also, r 2 2 = r 2 {tex}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex} With the help of Binomial theorem, keeping terms upto first order is shown below: {tex}\frac{1}{r_{1}} \equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{r_{2}} \equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} {tex}\equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} As p = qa {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q(2 a) \cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}V=\frac{p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex} Now, {tex}p \cos \theta=\vec{p}. \hat{r}{/tex} where {tex}\hat r{/tex} is unit vector along position vector. Hence electric potential of dipole for distances large compared to size of dipole is given as below : {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\vec p \hat r}{r^{2}}{/tex} for r >>a ---For potential at any point on axis, {tex}\theta=[0, \pi]{/tex} {tex}V=\pm \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r}{/tex} potential is positive when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 potential is negative when {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} Hence, electric potential falls at large distance, as {tex}\frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex} and not as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex}
