Hence electric potential of dipole for distances large compared to size of dipole is given as below : {tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\vec p \hat r}{r^{2}}{/tex} for r >>a ---For potential at any point on axis, {tex}\theta=[0, \pi]{/tex} {tex}V=\pm \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r}{/tex} potential is positive when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 potential is negative when {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} Hence, electric potential falls at large distance, as {tex}\frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex} and not as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider origin at the centre of dipole. As per superposition principle, potential due to dipole will be the sum of potentials due to charges q and -q
{tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{q}{r_{1}}-\frac{q}{r_{2}}\right]{/tex}
Where,
r1 and r2 = distances of point P from q and -q.
r12 = r2 + a2 - 2 arcos {tex}\theta{/tex}
r22 = r2 + a2 + 2 arcos {tex}\theta{/tex}
If r is greater than a, and taking terms upto first order in a/r
r12 = r2 {tex}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}+\frac{a^{2}}{r^{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=r^{2}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex}
Also, r22 = r2 {tex}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex}
With the help of Binomial theorem, keeping terms upto first order is shown below:
{tex}\frac{1}{r_{1}} \equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{r_{2}} \equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\equiv \frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex}
As p = qa
{tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{q(2 a) \cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}V=\frac{p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\cos \theta}{r^{2}}{/tex}
Now, {tex}p \cos \theta=\vec{p}. \hat{r}{/tex}
where {tex}\hat r{/tex} is unit vector along position vector.
Hence electric potential of dipole for distances large compared to size of dipole is given as below :
{tex}V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{\vec p \hat r}{r^{2}}{/tex} for r >>a

---For potential at any point on axis, {tex}\theta=[0, \pi]{/tex}

{tex}V=\pm \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{q}{r}{/tex}

potential is positive when {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
potential is negative when {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex}
Hence, electric potential falls at large distance, as {tex}\frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex} and not as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex}

1. If two similar large plates, each of area A having surface charge densities +{tex}\sigma{/tex} and - {tex}\sigma{/tex} are separated by a distance d in air, then find the expression for
    1. the field at points between the two plates and on the outer side of the plates. Specify the direction of the field in each case.
    2. the potential difference between the plates.
    3. the capacitance of the capacitor so formed.
  2. Two metallic spheres of radii R and 2R are charged so that both of these have the same surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex}. If they are connected to each other with a conducting wire, in which direction will the charge flow and why?
question_text: If two similar large plates, each of area A having surface charge densities + {tex}\sigma{/tex} and - {tex}\sigma{/tex} are separated by a distance d in air, then find the expression for the field at points between the two plates and on the outer side of the plates. Specify the direction of the field in each case. the potential difference between the plates. the capacitance of the capacitor so formed. Two metallic spheres of radii R and 2R are charged so that both of these have the same surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} . If they are connected to each other with a conducting wire, in which direction will the charge flow and why?
explanation: Consider the figure shown below: Electric field due to the plate of the positive charge of charge density {tex}+ \sigma{/tex} at point P, is given by E ​​​​​​1 = {tex}\sigma/2\varepsilon_0{/tex} Magnitude of electric field due to the other plate of negative charge density {tex}- \sigma{/tex} ,is given by E ​​​​​​2 = {tex}-\sigma/2\varepsilon_0{/tex} In , the inner region between the plates 1 and 2 , electric field due to the two charged plates add up, is given by {tex}E _ { \mathrm { net } } = E_1 + E_2 = \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } + \frac { \sigma } { 2 \varepsilon _ { 0 } } = \frac { \sigma } { \varepsilon _ { 0 } }{/tex} Outside the plate, electric field will be equal to zero because of the opposite directions of the electric fields E ​​​​​​1 and E ​​​​​​2 ​​​​​ there. Potential difference between the plates of the capacitor is given by V = Ed = {tex}\sigma d /\varepsilon_0{/tex} ( {tex}\because E = \sigma / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} ) Capacitance of the capacitor is given by ( {tex}\because{/tex} Q = CV) {tex}C = \frac { Q } { V } = \frac { \sigma A } { \sigma d } \varepsilon _ { 0 } = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} Consider the figure shown below: Potential at the surface of the sphere of radius R, {tex}= \frac { k q } { R } \quad \left[ \because q = \sigma \times 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} R ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \quad \frac { k \sigma 4 \pi R ^ { 2 } } { R } = \sigma k 4 \pi R = 4 k \sigma \pi R{/tex} Potential at the surface of the second sphere of radius twice the previous one i.e. 2R, {tex}= \frac { k q } { 2 R } \quad \left[ \because q = \sigma \times 4 \pi ( 2 R ) ^ { 2 } = 16 \sigma \pi R ^ { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { k 16 \sigma \pi R ^ { 2 } } { 2 R } = 8 k \sigma \pi R{/tex} We know that charge always flows from the higher potential surface to lower potential surface. Since the potential of the bigger sphere is more, so charge will flow from sphere of radius 2R to the sphere of radius R after connecting both the spheres by a conducting wire

      V = Ed = {tex}\sigma d /\varepsilon_0{/tex} ({tex}\because E = \sigma / \varepsilon _ { 0 }{/tex} )
    3. Capacitance of the capacitor is given by
       ({tex}\because{/tex} Q = CV)
      {tex}C = \frac { Q } { V } = \frac { \sigma A } { \sigma d } \varepsilon _ { 0 } = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex}

    We know that charge always flows from the higher potential surface to lower potential surface. Since the potential of the bigger sphere is more, so charge will flow from sphere of radius 2R to the sphere of radius R after connecting both the spheres by a conducting wire

question_html:
A capacitor of capacitance C1 is charged to a potential V1 while another capacitor of capacitance C2 is charged to a potential difference V2. The capacitors are now disconnected from their respective charging batteries and connected in parallel to each other.

  1. Find the total energy stored in the two capacitors before they are connected.
  2. Find the total energy stored in the parallel combination of two capacitors.
  3. Explain the reason for the difference of energy in parallel combination in comparison to the total energy before they are connected
question_text: A capacitor of capacitance C 1 is charged to a potential V 1 while another capacitor of capacitance C 2 is charged to a potential difference V 2 . The capacitors are now disconnected from their respective charging batteries and connected in parallel to each other. Find the total energy stored in the two capacitors before they are connected. Find the total energy stored in the parallel combination of two capacitors. Explain the reason for the difference of energy in parallel combination in comparison to the total energy before they are connected
explanation: Total energy stored in the two capacitors before they are connected, {tex}u_{i}=\frac{1}{2} C_{1} V_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} C_{2} V_{2}^{2}{/tex} After the two capacitors are connected in parallel, the common potential is {tex}V=\frac{\text { Total charge }}{\text { Total capacitance }}=\frac{q_{1}+q_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}=\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex} Total energy stored in the parallel combination, {tex}U_{f}=\frac{1}{2}\left(C_{1}+C_{2}\right) V^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(C_{1}+C_{2}\right)\left(\frac{C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}\right)^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left(C_{1} V_{1}+C_{2} V_{2}\right)^{2}}{C_{1}+C_{2}}{/tex} Clearly, U f < U i . Thus the total energy of the parallel combination is less than the sum of the energies stored in the two capacitors before they are connected. During sharing of charges, some energy is lost as heat due to the flow of charges in connecting wires.

question_text: Derive an expression for the electric potential at a point due to an electric dipole. Mention the contrasting features of electric potential of a dipole at a point as compared to that due to a single charge.
explanation: Consider an electric dipole having charges -q and +q at separation '2a'. The dipole moment of dipole is {tex}\vec{p}=q(\overrightarrow{2} a){/tex} , directed from -q to +q. The electric potential due to dipole is the algebraic sum of potentials due to charges +q to -q If r 1 and r 2 are distances of any point P from charge +q to -q respectively as shown in the figure, then the potential due to electric dipole at point P, is {tex} V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r_1}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r_2}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{1}{r_1}-\frac{1}{r_2}\right] {/tex} ...(i) If (r, {tex}\theta{/tex} ) are polar coordinates of point P with respect to mid-point O of dipole, then By geometry, {tex}r_1^2=r^2+a^2-2 a r \cos \theta{/tex} ...(ii) and, {tex}r_2^2=r^2+a^2-2 a r \cos \theta{/tex} ...(iii) From (ii), {tex} r_1^2=r^2\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}+\frac{a^2}{r^2}\right] {/tex} If r >> a i e., {tex}\frac{a}{r}{/tex} <<1, then it is sufficient to retain terms only upto first order in {tex}\left(\frac{a}{r}\right){/tex} . {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} r_1^2=r^2\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow r_1=r\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac 1 2} {/tex} ...(iv) Similarly from (iii), {tex}r_2{ }^2=r^2\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right] {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r_2=r\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac 1 2}{/tex} ...(v) From (iv) and (v), {tex}\frac{1}{r_1}=\frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac {-1} 2}{/tex} and, {tex}\frac{1}{r_2}=\frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac {-1} 2}{/tex} Using binomial theorem and retaining terms upto first order in {tex}\left(\frac{a}{r}\right){/tex} only, we have {tex}\frac{1}{r_1}=\frac{1}{r}\left[1-\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} ...(vi) and, {tex} \frac{1}{r_2}=\frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right] {/tex} ...(vii) Substituting these values in (i), we get {tex} V=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{1}{r}\left(1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right)-\frac{1}{r}\left(1-\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right)\right] {/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r}{/tex} {tex}\left[1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta-1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r}\left[\frac{2 a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(q \cdot 2 a) \cos \theta}{r^2}{/tex} or, {tex} V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p \cos \theta}{r^2} {/tex} ...(viii) But, {tex}p \cos \theta=\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}{/tex} where, {tex}\hat{r}{/tex} is unit vector along position vector {tex}\vec{O} P=\vec{r}{/tex} . Electric potential due to an electric dipole is {tex} V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}}{r^2}{/tex} (for r >> a) {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{r}}{r^3} {/tex} ...(ix) Contrasting features: The electric potential due to a dipole depends on distance r and also on the angle between position vector {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} and dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} . The electrostatic potential at large distances falls off, as {tex}\frac{1}{r^2}{/tex} and not as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex} which is the characteristic of potential due to a single charge. Special Cases: When point P lies on the axis of dipole, then {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 0 = 1 {tex}\therefore V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{r^2}{/tex} When point P lies on the equatorial plane of the dipole, then {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 90 o = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = 0 It may be noted that the electric potential at any point on the equitorial line of a dipole is zero.

Special Cases: When point P lies on the axis of dipole, then {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0 o {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 0 = 1 {tex}\therefore V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{r^2}{/tex} When point P lies on the equatorial plane of the dipole, then {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 90 o = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = 0 It may be noted that the electric potential at any point on the equitorial line of a dipole is zero.
solution_html:
Consider an electric dipole having charges -q and +q at separation '2a'. The dipole moment of dipole is {tex}\vec{p}=q(\overrightarrow{2} a){/tex}, directed from -q to +q.
The electric potential due to dipole is the algebraic sum of potentials due to charges +q to -q
If r1 and r2 are distances of any point P from charge +q to -q respectively as shown in the figure, then the potential due to electric dipole at point P, is

{tex} V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r_1}-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r_2}=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\frac{1}{r_1}-\frac{1}{r_2}\right] {/tex} ...(i)
If (r, {tex}\theta{/tex}) are polar coordinates of point P with respect to mid-point O of dipole, then
By geometry,
{tex}r_1^2=r^2+a^2-2 a r \cos \theta{/tex} ...(ii)
and, {tex}r_2^2=r^2+a^2-2 a r \cos \theta{/tex}...(iii)
From (ii), {tex} r_1^2=r^2\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}+\frac{a^2}{r^2}\right] {/tex}
If r >> a i e.,{tex}\frac{a}{r}{/tex} <<1, then it is sufficient to retain terms only upto first order in {tex}\left(\frac{a}{r}\right){/tex}.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} r_1^2=r^2\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow r_1=r\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac 1 2} {/tex} ...(iv)
Similarly from (iii), {tex}r_2{ }^2=r^2\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right] {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r_2=r\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac 1 2}{/tex} ...(v)
From (iv) and (v), {tex}\frac{1}{r_1}=\frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac {-1} 2}{/tex} and, {tex}\frac{1}{r_2}=\frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]^{\frac {-1} 2}{/tex}
Using binomial theorem and retaining terms upto first order in {tex}\left(\frac{a}{r}\right){/tex} only, we have
{tex}\frac{1}{r_1}=\frac{1}{r}\left[1-\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{2 a \cos \theta}{r}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{r}\left[1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} ...(vi)
and, {tex} \frac{1}{r_2}=\frac{1}{r}\left[1-\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right] {/tex} ...(vii)
Substituting these values in (i), we get
{tex} V=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{1}{r}\left(1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right)-\frac{1}{r}\left(1-\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right)\right] {/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r}{/tex} {tex}\left[1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta-1+\frac{a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r}\left[\frac{2 a}{r} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(q \cdot 2 a) \cos \theta}{r^2}{/tex}
or, {tex} V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p \cos \theta}{r^2} {/tex} ...(viii)
But, {tex}p \cos \theta=\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}{/tex} where, {tex}\hat{r}{/tex} is unit vector along position vector {tex}\vec{O} P=\vec{r}{/tex}.
Electric potential due to an electric dipole is
{tex} V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \hat{r}}{r^2}{/tex} (for r >> a) {tex}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{\vec{p} \cdot \vec{r}}{r^3} {/tex} ...(ix)
Contrasting features: The electric potential due to a dipole depends on distance r and also on the angle between position vector {tex}\vec{r}{/tex} and dipole moment {tex}\vec{p}{/tex}. The electrostatic potential at large distances falls off, as {tex}\frac{1}{r^2}{/tex} and not as {tex}\frac{1}{r}{/tex} which is the characteristic of potential due to a single charge.
Special Cases:

  1. When point P lies on the axis of dipole, then {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0o
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 0 = 1
    {tex}\therefore V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{p}{r^2}{/tex}
  2. When point P lies on the equatorial plane of the dipole, then
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 90o = 0
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = 0
    It may be noted that the electric potential at any point on the equitorial line of a dipole is zero.

question_html:
1. Deduce the expression for the energy stored in a charged capacitor
  2. Show that the effective capacitance C of a series combination of three capacitors C1, C2 and C3 is given by {tex}\frac1C=\;\frac1{C_1}+\frac1{C_2}+\frac1{C_3}{/tex}.
question_text: Deduce the expression for the energy stored in a charged capacitor Show that the effective capacitance C of a series combination of three capacitors C 1 , C 2 and C 3 is given by {tex}\frac1C=\;\frac1{C_1}+\frac1{C_2}+\frac1{C_3}{/tex} .
explanation: During charging of the capacitor, work is done by the battery which is stored in the form of potential energy inside the capacitor. Consider a capacitor which is to be charged by charge Q with the help of a battery. Let at any instant charge on the capacitor is q and the potential difference between the two plates of the capacitor is V. We know that, q = CV {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = q/C Now small work done to charge the capacitor by small charge dq, dW = Vdq = {tex}\frac { q } { C } d q{/tex} where, q = instantaneous charge, C = capacitance and V = voltage {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in storing charge from 0 to Q(total charge) is given by {tex}\Rightarrow W=\int_0^Q\frac qCdq\;=\frac{Q^2}{2C}{/tex} In a series combination of capacitors, the same charge lie on each capacitor for any value of capacitances. Also, the net potential difference across the combination is equal to the algebraic sum of potential differences across each capacitor i.e. V = V 1 + V 2 + V 3 ..........(i) where V 1 , V 2 , V 3 and V are the potential differences across C 1 , C 2 , C 3 and equivalent capacitor, respectively. Again {tex}q_1 =C_1 V_1 \Rightarrow V_1 = \frac {q_1}{C_1}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } ~and~ V _ { 3 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total potential difference [From Eq.(i)] {tex}\Rightarrow V = \frac { q } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C }{/tex} , where C is equivalent capacitance]

Consider a capacitor which is to be charged by charge Q with the help of a battery. Let at any instant charge on the capacitor is q and the potential difference between the two plates of the capacitor is V. We know that, q = CV {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = q/C Now small work done to charge the capacitor by small charge dq, dW = Vdq = {tex}\frac { q } { C } d q{/tex} where, q = instantaneous charge, C = capacitance and V = voltage {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in storing charge from 0 to Q(total charge) is given by {tex}\Rightarrow W=\int_0^Q\frac qCdq\;=\frac{Q^2}{2C}{/tex} In a series combination of capacitors, the same charge lie on each capacitor for any value of capacitances. Also, the net potential difference across the combination is equal to the algebraic sum of potential differences across each capacitor i.e. V = V 1 + V 2 + V 3 ..........(i) where V 1 , V 2 , V 3 and V are the potential differences across C 1 , C 2 , C 3 and equivalent capacitor, respectively. Again {tex}q_1 =C_1 V_1 \Rightarrow V_1 = \frac {q_1}{C_1}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } ~and~ V _ { 3 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total potential difference [From Eq.(i)] {tex}\Rightarrow V = \frac { q } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C }{/tex} , where C is equivalent capacitance]
solution_html:
1. During charging of the capacitor, work is done by the battery which is stored in the form of potential energy inside the capacitor.
    Consider a capacitor which is to be charged by charge Q with the help of a battery. Let at any instant charge on the capacitor is q and the potential difference between the two plates of the capacitor is V.
    We know that,
    q = CV {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = q/C
    Now small work done to charge the capacitor by small charge dq,
    dW = Vdq = {tex}\frac { q } { C } d q{/tex}
    where, q = instantaneous charge, C = capacitance and  V = voltage
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in storing charge from 0 to Q(total charge) is given by
    {tex}\Rightarrow W=\int_0^Q\frac qCdq\;=\frac{Q^2}{2C}{/tex}

  2. In a series combination of capacitors, the same charge lie on each capacitor for any value of capacitances.

    Also, the net potential difference across the combination is equal to the algebraic sum of potential differences across each capacitor
    i.e. V = V1 + V2 + V3 ..........(i)
    where V1 , V2, V3 and V are the potential differences across C1, C2, C3 and equivalent capacitor, respectively.
    Again {tex}q_1 =C_1 V_1 \Rightarrow V_1 = \frac {q_1}{C_1}{/tex}
    Similarly, {tex}V _ { 2 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } ~and~ V _ { 3 } = \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex}
     {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total potential difference [From Eq.(i)]
    {tex}\Rightarrow V = \frac { q } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { q } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { C } = \frac { 1 } { C _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { C _ { 3 } }{/tex} [ {tex}\frac { V } { q } = \frac { 1 } { C }{/tex} , where C is equivalent capacitance]

question_html:
1. Derive the expression for the energy stored in parallel plate capacitor. Hence, obtain the expression for the energy density of the electric field.
  2. A fully charged parallel plate capacitor is connected across an uncharged identical capacitor. Show that the energy stored in the combination is less than the energy stored initially in the single capacitor.
question_text: Derive the expression for the energy stored in parallel plate capacitor. Hence, obtain the expression for the energy density of the electric field. A fully charged parallel plate capacitor is connected across an uncharged identical capacitor. Show that the energy stored in the combination is less than the energy stored initially in the single capacitor.
explanation: Let the total charge on the plates of the below capacitor is +Q and -Q respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} The potential difference between the plates of the above capacitor of capacitance C for an infinitesimal charge q is q/C. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Potential of condenser = q/C Small amount of work done in giving an additional charge dq to the condenser, {tex}d W = \frac { q } { C } \times d q{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total work done in giving a charge Q to the condenser, {tex}W = \int _ { q = 0 } ^ { q = Q }{\frac {q}{C} dq} = \frac { 1 } { C } \left[ \frac { q ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { q = 0 } ^ { q = Q } \Rightarrow W = \frac { 1 } { C } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { 2 }{/tex} As, an electrostatic force is conservative, this work is stored in the form of potential energy (U) of the condenser. {tex}U = W = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad Q = C V \Rightarrow U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { ( C V ) ^ { 2 } } { C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad C V = Q \Rightarrow U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} Hence, {tex}U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { Q ^ { 2 } } { C } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } Q V{/tex} Energy density (u) is defined as the total energy per unit volume of the condenser. i.e., {tex}u = \frac { \text { Total energy } ( U ) } { \text { Volume } ( V ) } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 } } { A d }{/tex} Using, {tex}C = \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d }{/tex} and V = Ed (Where V is the potential difference and E is the Electric field existing between the plates) We get, {tex}u = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \varepsilon _ { 0 } A } { d } \right) \left( \frac { E ^ { 2 } d ^ { 2 } } { A d } \right)= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } E ^ { 2 }{/tex} Here, Energy density between plates of capacitors is directly proportional to electric field that exists between the plates of capacitor. Initial condition : If we consider a charged capacitor of capacitance C with potential difference V, then its charge would be given, q = CV and energy stored in it is given by {tex}U _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} .....(i) When this charged capacitor is connected to uncharged capacitor, Let the common potential be V 1 , the charge flow from first capacitor to the other capacitor unless both the capacitor attains the common potential. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Q 1 = CV 1 and Q 2 = CV 2 Applying conservation of charge, Q = Q 1 + Q 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} CV = CV 1 + CV 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V = V 1 + V 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V 1 = {tex}\frac { V } { 2 }{/tex} [hence voltage will be equally divided between the capacitors] Total energy stored in both the capacitor is {tex}U _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow U _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C \left( \frac { V } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C \left( \frac { V } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}U _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 C V ^ { 2 } } { 8 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } C V ^ { 2 }{/tex} From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, U 2 < U 1 It means that energy stored in the combination is less than that stored initially in the single capacitor. It is due to the fact that when the charge is transferred from one capacitor to another capacitor energy is wasted in transferring the charge.

(i) and (ii), we get, U 2 < U 1 It means that energy stored in the combination is less than that stored initially in the single capacitor. It is due to the fact that when the charge is transferred from one capacitor to another capacitor energy is wasted in transferring the charge.

question_html:
Derive an expression for equivalent capacitance of three capacitors when connected

  1. in series and
  2. in parallel.
question_text: Derive an expression for equivalent capacitance of three capacitors when connected in series and in parallel.
explanation: In fig. (a) three capacitors of capacitances C 1 , C 2 , C 3 are connected in series between points A and D. In series first plate of each capacitor has charge +Q and second plate of each capacitor has charge -Q i.e., charge on each capacitor is Q. Let the potential differences across the capacitors C 1 , C 2 , C 3 be V 1 , V 2 , V 3 respectively. As the second plate of first capacitor C 1 and first plate of second capacitor C 2 are connected together, their potentials are equal. Let this common potential be V B . Similarly the common potential of second plate of C 2 and first plate of C 3 is V C . The second plate of capacitor C 3 is connected to earth, therefore its potential V D = 0. As charge flows from higher potential to lower potential, therefore V A > V B > V C > V D . For the first capacitor, V 1 = V A - V B {tex}=\frac{Q}{C_1}{/tex} ...(i) For the second capacitor, V 2 = V B - V C {tex}= \frac{Q}{C_2}{/tex} ...(ii) For the third capacitor, V 3 = V C - V D {tex}= \frac{Q}{C_3}{/tex} ...(iii) Adding (i), (ii) and (iii), we get V 1 + V 2 + V 3 =V A - V D {tex}=Q\left[\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}\right]{/tex} ...(iv) If V be the potential difference between A and D, then V A - V D = V {tex}\therefore{/tex} From (iv), we get V = (V 1 + V 2 + V 3 ) {tex}=Q\left[\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}\right]{/tex} ...(v) If in place of all the three capacitors, only one capacitor is placed between A and D such that on giving it charge Q, the potential difference between its plates become V, then it will be called equivalent capacitor . If its capacitance is C, then V = {tex}\frac Q C{/tex} ...(vi) Comparing (v) and (vi), we get {tex}\frac{Q}{C}=Q\left[\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}\right]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}{/tex} ...(vii) Thus in series arrangement, "The reciprocal of equivalent capacitance is equal to the sum of the reciprocals of the individual capacitors." Parallel Arrangement: In fig. (c) three capacitors of capacitance C 1 , C 2 , C 3 are connected in parallel. In parallel the potential difference across each capacitor is same V (say). solution_html:
1. In fig. (a) three capacitors of capacitances C1, C2, C3 are connected in series between points A and D.
     
    In series first plate of each capacitor has charge +Q and second plate of each capacitor has charge -Q i.e., charge on each capacitor is Q.
    Let the potential differences across the capacitors C1, C2, C3 be V1, V2, V3 respectively. As the second plate of first capacitor C1 and first plate of second capacitor C2 are connected together, their potentials are equal. Let this common potential be VB. Similarly the common potential of second plate of C2 and first plate of C3 is VC. The second plate of capacitor C3 is connected to earth, therefore its potential VD = 0. As charge flows from higher potential to lower potential, therefore VA > VB > VC > VD.
    For the first capacitor, V1 = VA - VB {tex}=\frac{Q}{C_1}{/tex} ...(i)
    For the second capacitor, V2 = VB - VC {tex}= \frac{Q}{C_2}{/tex} ...(ii)
    For the third capacitor, V3 = VC - VD {tex}= \frac{Q}{C_3}{/tex} ...(iii)
    Adding (i), (ii) and (iii), we get
    V1 + V2 + V3 =VA - VD {tex}=Q\left[\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}\right]{/tex} ...(iv)
    If V be the potential difference between A and D, then
    VA - VD = V
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} From (iv), we get
    V = (V1 + V2 + V3) {tex}=Q\left[\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}\right]{/tex} ...(v)
    If in place of all the three capacitors, only one capacitor is placed between A and D such that on giving it charge Q, the potential difference between its plates become V, then it will be called equivalent capacitor. If its capacitance is C, then
    V = {tex}\frac Q C{/tex} ...(vi)
    Comparing (v) and (vi), we get
    {tex}\frac{Q}{C}=Q\left[\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}\right]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{1}{C}=\frac{1}{C_1}+\frac{1}{C_2}+\frac{1}{C_3}{/tex} ...(vii)
    Thus in series arrangement, "The reciprocal of equivalent capacitance is equal to the sum of the reciprocals of the individual capacitors."
  2. Parallel Arrangement: In fig. (c) three capacitors of capacitance C1, C2, C3 are connected in parallel.
      

    In parallel the potential difference across each capacitor is same V (say). Clearly the potential difference between plates of each capacitor
    VA - VB = V (say)
    The charge Q given to capacitors is divided on capacitors C1, C2, C3.
    Let q1, q2, q3 be the charges on capacitors C1, C2, C3 respectively.
    Then Q = q1 + q2 + q3 ...(I)
    and q1 = C1V,q2 = C2V,q3 = C3V
    Substituting these values in (i), we get
    Q = C1V + C2V + C3V or Q = (C1 + C2 + C3)V ...(ii)
    If, in place of all the three capacitors, only one capacitor of capacitance C be connected between A and B; such that on giving it charge Q the potential difference between its plates be V, then it will be called equivalent capacitor. If C be the capacitance of equivalent capacitor, then
    Q = CV ...(iii)
    Comparing equations (ii) and (iii), we get
    CV = (C1 + C2 + C3)V or C = (C1 + C2 + C3)
    Important Note: It may be noted carefully that the formula for the total capacitance in series and parallel combination of capacitors is the reverse of corresponding formula for combination of resistors in current electricity.

Now consider a ring of radius r of thickness dr on a disc of radius R, as shown in the figure, Let us consider disc is divided into a large number of rings, Again let the charge on the ring is dq then potential dV due to ring at P, will be {tex}d V=\frac{k d q}{r^{\prime}}\left[\because r^{\prime}=\sqrt{r^{2}+x^{2}}\right]{/tex} dq is the charge on the ring = {tex}\sigma {/tex} area of ring {tex}=\sigma \cdot\left[\pi(r+d r)^{2}-\pi r^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}d q=\sigma \cdot \pi\left[r^{2}+d r^{2}+2 r d r-r^{2}\right]{/tex} Because dr is small, therefore, dr 2 is negligible. {tex}\therefore \mathrm{d} \mathrm{q}=\sigma \pi(2 r d r)=2 \pi r \sigma \cdot d r{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathrm{d} \mathrm{V}=\frac{ k.2 \pi r \sigma d r}{\sqrt{\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} So the potential due to charged disc {tex}\int_{0}^{V} d V=\int_{0}^{R} \frac{k. 2 \pi \sigma d r}{\sqrt{r^{2}+x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}V=k.2 \pi \sigma \cdot \int_{0}^{R} \frac{r d r}{\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{12}}=k. \pi \sigma \int_{0}^{R} r \cdot\left(r^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{-1 / 2} 2 d r{/tex} = {tex}k \pi\sigma[\sqrt{r^2+x^2}]^R_0\over {1/2}{/tex} {tex}=2 \pi k \sigma\left[\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}-x\right]=\frac{2 \pi \sigma}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left[\left(R^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}-x\right]{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} R 2 {tex}\sigma{/tex} = Q(charge on disc) {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi2 R^{2} \sigma}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} R^{2}}[\sqrt{R^{2}+x^{2}}-x]{/tex} thus the potential due to a disc is given by, {tex}V=\frac{2 Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon _0R^{2}}[\sqrt{R^{2}+x^{2}}-x]{/tex}
